core_question: Should we invest in or develop a strategy around nuclear fusion energy, and if so, when and in which segments?
central_thesis: The storyline should be led by the strongest public evidence, then translated into management choices.
narrative_focus: Evidence is densest around: funding, timeline, adoption, technology
structure_logic: Open with the executive decision, show source-backed data exhibits, then move through business implications, risks and action gates.
evidence_must_cover: To date, Milestone awardees have collectively raised over $350 million of new private funding since their selection into the program was announced in May 2023, compared to the $46 million of federal funding initially committed for negotiated milestones.

To date, Milestone awardees have collectively raised over $350 million of new private funding since their selection into the program was announced in May 2023, compared to the $46 million of federal funding initially committed for negotiated milestones.

Highlights of the FY 2027 Request The FES FY 2027 Request of $755.3 million is a decrease of $50.406 million below FY 2026 Enacted, with reduced funding for core research to offset increases for high priority research activities and facility operations.

Highlights of the FY 2027 Request The FES FY 2027 Request of $755.3 million is a decrease of $50.406 million below FY 2026 Enacted, with reduced funding for core research to offset increases for high priority research activities and facility operations.

All 8 awardees are presently working toward presenting pre-conceptual designs and technology roadmaps of their FPP concepts within the first 18 months of the Milestone program—roughly late 2025 (18 months into the Milestone Program).

Department of Energy (DOE) today announced $107 million in funding for six projects in the Fusion Innovative Research Engine (FIRE) Collaboratives , and that several privately funded fusion companies have completed early critical-path science and technology (S&T) milestones in the Milestone-Based Fusion Development Program (“the Milestone Program”).

hypotheses_to_test.hypothesis: Nuclear fusion will not achieve grid-connected electricity generation before 2035.
hypotheses_to_test.decision_relevance: Determines investment horizon and need for interim technologies.
needed_evidence: Published timelines from major fusion projects (ITER, SPARC, etc.) and independent assessments.

hypotheses_to_test.hypothesis: First-of-a-kind fusion plants will have LCOE > $100/MWh, making them uncompetitive with renewables + storage by 2035.
hypotheses_to_test.decision_relevance: Affects economic justification for early adoption.
needed_evidence: LCOE estimates from fusion developers and independent studies; renewable LCOE projections.

hypotheses_to_test.hypothesis: Private fusion funding will exceed $10 billion cumulatively by 2025, but less than 20% of startups will reach a working prototype.
hypotheses_to_test.decision_relevance: Indicates market confidence and survival rate.
needed_evidence: Funding data from Crunchbase, PitchBook; progress reports from fusion companies.

hypotheses_to_test.hypothesis: Government policy support (e.g., tax credits, regulatory sandboxes) is necessary for fusion commercialization before 2040.
needed_evidence: Policy announcements from US, UK, Japan; fusion-specific legislation.

hypotheses_to_test.hypothesis: Fusion will first be commercialized in niche applications (e.g., industrial heat, marine propulsion) rather than baseload electricity.
hypotheses_to_test.decision_relevance: Shifts market focus from power generation to specialized uses.
needed_evidence: Developer roadmaps targeting non-power applications; market size for industrial heat.

hypotheses_to_test.hypothesis: The fusion supply chain (e.g., tritium, superconducting magnets) will face bottlenecks that delay commercialization by 5+ years.
hypotheses_to_test.decision_relevance: Identifies critical dependencies and investment opportunities.
needed_evidence: Tritium availability studies; magnet production capacity; supply chain risk assessments.

hypotheses_to_test.hypothesis: By 2030, fusion energy will attract more private investment than nuclear fission, but less than solar and wind.
hypotheses_to_test.decision_relevance: Benchmarks fusion against competing clean energy technologies.
needed_evidence: Investment data for fusion, fission, solar, wind from 2020-2030 projections.

market_sizing_logic.formula: Total addressable market (TAM) = Global electricity generation (kWh) × fusion penetration rate × price per kWh
known_limitations: Penetration rate is highly uncertain; assumes fusion is cost-competitive.

market_sizing_logic.formula: Market size = Number of fusion plants × average capacity (MW) × capacity factor × price per MWh
variables: Number of plants (based on announced projects and realistic build rates)
known_limitations: Few announced projects; many may not materialize.

market_sizing_logic.formula: Market size = (Number of potential customers) × (adoption rate at each stage) × (revenue per customer)
variables: Potential customers: utilities, industrial users, governments
Adoption stages: awareness, interest, trial, adoption
Revenue per customer: plant sale or PPA value
known_limitations: Adoption rates are speculative; limited data on customer intent.

market_sizing_logic.formula: Value pool = Sum of profits across value chain (components, construction, fuel, O&M, electricity sales)
known_limitations: Value chain not yet established; many costs are estimates.

charting_rule: Charts may use only evidence_ledger values, dates, source counts or same-unit comparable values tied to public URLs.
